hook: You already know how to solve a system of two equations by substitution or elimination — add equations together, cancel a variable, and back-solve. But what happens when you have four equations and five unknowns, or when the system has no solution or infinitely many? Doing ad-hoc elimination quickly becomes chaotic and error-prone. Is there a systematic, algorithmic way to bring any linear system to a form where the solution (or lack thereof) is completely transparent?
concept: Row reduction is a systematic procedure for simplifying a matrix by applying elementary row operations until it reaches a standardized 'staircase' shape called echelon form (or the even simpler reduced row echelon form). Formally, a matrix is in row echelon form (REF) if every nonzero row lies above all zero rows, and the leading entry (called a pivot) of each nonzero row is strictly to the right of the pivot in the row above it. In reduced row echelon form (RREF) each pivot equals $1$ and is the only nonzero entry in its column.
anatomy: The key structural pieces of row reduction are: (1) the augmented matrix $[A | \mathbf{b}]$, formed by appending the right-hand-side vector $\mathbf{b}$ to the coefficient matrix $A$; (2) the three elementary row operations — row swap $R_i \leftrightarrow R_j$, row scaling $R_i \leftarrow c R_i$ ($c \neq 0$), and row replacement $R_i \leftarrow R_i + c R_j$; (3) the pivot position, the leading nonzero entry used to eliminate entries below (and in RREF, above) it; and (4) free variables, which correspond to non-pivot columns and parametrize the solution set when infinitely many solutions exist.

worked_example.description_latex: We begin by converting the linear system into its augmented matrix $[A|\mathbf{b}]$, which packages all the coefficients and right-hand sides into one object we can manipulate row by row. Separating the system into a matrix lets us focus purely on the numbers and apply operations mechanically, without rewriting variable names at every step.

We use the pivot in position $(1,1)$, which is the entry $1$ in row 1, to eliminate the $x_1$ terms from all rows below it. Subtracting $2$ times row 1 from row 2 zeroes out the $2$ in row 2, and adding row 1 to row 3 zeroes out the $-1$ in row 3 — this is the row replacement operation and it preserves the solution set because we are adding a multiple of one equation to another.

Now we pivot on the $-1$ in position $(2,2)$, using it to eliminate the $3$ in row 3, column 2. Adding $3$ times row 2 to row 3 achieves this, giving us the upper-triangular staircase pattern that defines row echelon form — every row below the current pivot has a zero in that column.

To move toward RREF we scale each pivot row so its leading entry becomes exactly $1$. Multiplying row 2 by $-1$ and row 3 by $\frac{1}{10}$ accomplishes this without changing the solution set, since scaling a row by a nonzero constant is a valid elementary operation.

worked_example.expression_latex: R_2 \leftarrow R_2 + 3R_3, \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 + R_3, \quad R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - 2R_2 \longrightarrow \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1}{5} \\ 0 & 1 & 0 & \tfrac{3}{5} \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \tfrac{6}{5} \end{array}\right]
worked_example.description_latex: Finally, we back-eliminate upward using each pivot to zero out all entries above it — this is what distinguishes RREF from plain REF. Reading off the last column directly gives the unique solution $x_1 = \frac{1}{5},\ x_2 = \frac{3}{5},\ x_3 = \frac{6}{5}$, with no substitution required because the matrix is now in RREF.

partial_example.description_latex: We start by writing the augmented matrix. Notice that the easier pivot to work with would be a leading $1$, so it is often smart to swap or scale before eliminating.
We swap the two rows so that the leading $1$ is in the pivot position, making the next elimination step cleaner. Now apply a row replacement to eliminate the $2$ in row 2, column 1.
Apply the operation $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 - 2R_1$ and write the resulting augmented matrix. This is the core elimination step — what does row 2 become after you subtract twice row 1 from it?
Scale row 2 to make its pivot equal to $1$, then back-eliminate to reach RREF and read off the values of $x_1$ and $x_2$. Write the final RREF matrix and state the solution.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Bring the following matrix to row echelon form (REF): $\left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 2 & 6 & 10 \end{array}\right]$
practice_problems.answer_latex: \left[\begin{array}{cc|c} 1 & 3 & 5 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]$; the system has infinitely many solutions ($x_2$ is free).

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Use row reduction to solve the system: $\begin{cases} x + y = 4 \\ 3x - y = 8 \end{cases}$
Reduce the following augmented matrix to RREF and identify pivot columns: $\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 0 & -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]$
practice_problems.answer_latex: \text{RREF: } \left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 0 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]; \text{ pivot columns 1 and 2; } x_3 \text{ is free.}

practice_problems.prompt_latex: A system has augmented matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c} 1 & 2 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & -1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & k \end{array}\right]$. For what value(s) of $k$ is the system consistent, and what is the solution when consistent?
practice_problems.answer_latex: k = 0; \text{ when } k=0: \ x_1 = 1 + 2t,\ x_2 = 1 + t,\ x_3 = t \text{ for any } t \in \mathbb{R}.

practice_problems.prompt_latex: Three friends pool their money. Together, all three have $\$60$. The first and second together have $\$40$, and the second and third together have $\$35$. Set up the augmented matrix and use row reduction to find how much each person has.

common_mistakes: Students perform the row replacement $R_i \leftarrow R_i + c R_j$ but accidentally overwrite the row they are using as the source (i.e., they update $R_j$ mid-calculation and then use the new version for a later step in the same pass). Always complete all operations involving $R_j$ in a single round before modifying it.

When a zero appears on the diagonal, students try to use it as a pivot, leading to division by zero or leaving the matrix in a non-echelon form. The fix is to look for a nonzero entry below the zero and perform a row swap to bring a valid pivot into position before continuing.

Students confuse row echelon form with reduced row echelon form and stop eliminating once they reach an upper-triangular shape, then back-substitute manually — and make algebra errors. Remember that RREF requires eliminating upward as well, so each pivot column contains exactly one nonzero entry, and the solution can be read directly without any further substitution.
untested_variants: Systems where the number of equations exceeds the number of unknowns (overdetermined systems), which may be inconsistent or have a unique solution depending on the extra constraint.

Row reducing a matrix that is not augmented — for example, finding the rank of a coefficient matrix $A$ alone, or determining whether a set of vectors is linearly independent by row reducing the matrix whose columns are those vectors.
Using row reduction to compute the inverse of an invertible matrix via the algorithm $[A | I] \to [I | A^{-1}]$, which is a major application of RREF not covered in these problems.
Parametric solution sets with two or more free variables (two non-pivot columns), requiring students to express $x_1$ and $x_2$ each in terms of two free parameters $s$ and $t$.
